title: ⚔️ Terraform Workspaces vs Environments — Part 7
summary: Guide comparing Terraform workspaces (single config with multiple state files) against environment-based structures (separate folders) for infrastructure management. Workspaces simplify code reuse but lack clarity for teams, while environment structures scale better for production systems. Recommends environment-based approach for production and team-based deployments.
content:
So far in this series, we’ve:
Deployed infrastructure with Terraform
Used variables and outputs
Built reusable modules
Implemented remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB)
Structured projects using dev / staging / prod
Now, there’s an important question 👇
👉 Should you use Terraform Workspaces instead of environments?
Let’s break it down.
In this guide:
What Terraform workspaces are
How they work
Differences between workspaces and environments
When to use each approach
A workspace is:
👉 A way to manage multiple state files using the same Terraform configuration
By default, Terraform uses:
terraform workspace list

Output:
default

terraform workspace new dev
terraform workspace new prod

Switch between them:
terraform workspace select dev

👉 Each workspace represents a separate state.
Terraform stores state per workspace:
terraform.tfstate.d/
  dev/
  prod/

👉 Same code
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  instance_type = terraform.workspace == "prod" ? "t3.medium" : "t2.micro"
}

👉 Behavior changes based on workspace.
Workspaces allow you to:
Avoid duplicating code
Manage multiple environments quickly
Workspaces seem simple… but in real-world projects 👇
❌ Not very clear for teams
From Part 6:
environments/
  dev/
  staging/
  prod/

👉 Each environment has:
Separate folder
Separate backend
Clear structure
Let’s compare 👇
Workspaces → simple but limited
Environments → structured and scalable
👉 Workspaces are useful for:
Quick testing
Personal projects
👉 Environments are better for:
Production systems
Team collaboration
Long-term infrastructure
👉 Use environment-based structure for real-world projects
👉 Use workspaces only for simple use cases
Do not rely only on workspaces for:
Production infrastructure
Team-based deployments
👉 It increases risk.
How Terraform workspaces work
Their advantages and limitations
Why environments are preferred in production
Terraform gives you multiple ways to solve a problem.
👉 But not all approaches scale.
Choosing the right struct

zh.title: ⚔️ Terraform 工作区 vs 环境 — 第7部分
zh.summary: 指南对比 Terraform 工作区（单个配置文件配合多个状态文件）与基于环境的结构（分离的文件夹）用于基础设施管理。工作区简化代码复用但对团队缺乏清晰度，而环境结构对生产系统的可扩展性更好。建议在生产环境和基于团队的部署中采用基于环境的方法。

title: The Uncomfortable Truth About Loyalty At Your First Job 💸
summary: Early-career software engineers face stagnant salary growth through loyalty, with typical annual raises capped at 3-5%, while external job changes offer 20% compensation increases. The mismatch stems from companies budgeting conservatively for internal raises but paying market rate for new hires. Understanding this structural reality helps early-career engineers navigate compensation strategies without attributing gaps to manager unfairness.
content:
You know that feeling when you’ve been at a company for two years, you’re doing great work, and your raise comes in at… 4%?
Meanwhile, your college friend who jumped ship after 14 months just got a 20% bump in total comp.
That stings. And it teaches you something important early: loyalty is rarely rewarded in your first few years in tech.
I learned this the hard way. Let’s break it down 👇
When I started my career as a software engineer at Netskope, I thought the playbook was simple. 
Work hard, be reliable, and the company will take care of you. 
That’s what everyone tells us growing up. 
And look, Netskope was a great experience. I learned a ton. But after about a year, I made the move to Amazon and saw something eye-opening: the biggest jump in my compensation didn’t come from being at the same place for another year. It came from changing jobs. 
Not just in pay. But also in the kind of problems I got to solve.
Staying put would have been comfortable, but comfort never leads to growth.
Subscribe to get posts like this in your inbox
I have come to understand that this isn’t about your manager being unfair. It’s just a structure everyone follows.

Most companies budget 3-5% for annual raises across the board. That’s the pool. Your manager might fight to get you 7%, and that’s considered exceptional internally. 
But when a company is hiring externally? They’re paying market rate. And market rate moves a lot faster than 3-5% a year, especially in your first decade in tech when your skills are compounding quickly.
So you end up in this weird situation where the new hire sitting next to you, doing the same job, might be making $20-30K more. 
Simply because they negotiated a fresh offer while you’ve been collecting incremental raises.
Companies know this. 
But they also know that most people won’t leave. 
Inertia is powerful. You like your team, you’re mid-project, you don’t want to interview. They’re betting on that.
I’m not saying quit every two years like clockwork.

zh.title: 关于你第一份工作中的忠诚的不舒适真相 💸
zh.summary: 早期职业软件工程师因忠诚面临工资增长停滞，年度加薪上限通常为3-5%，而外部工作变化提供20%的薪酬增长。这种差异源于公司对内部加薪的保守预算，但对新员工支付市场价格。理解这一结构性现实帮助早期职业工程师制定薪酬策略，无需将差异归咎于经理的不公平。

title: Cx Dev Log — 2026-04-12
summary: The Cx compiler project added support for infinite loops, break, and continue statements via a new LoopContext structure that properly manages control flow graphs and SSA bindings. The changes fix critical bugs in nested control flow handling and redirect existing tests toward improved language features. These substantial modifications await integration into the main branch after a three-day development period.
content:
Two notable changes hit submain today, breaking a three-day streak of quiet. The Phase 10 IR lowering now covers infinite loop, break, and continue, and a fresh process document defines the agent's operating approach. While both await integration into main, these updates provide key insights into language design and team workflow.
The real highlight is the bcd49f2 commit. Modifications in src/ir/lower.rs, with 215 lines added and 16 removed, introduce a LoopContext structure that guides IR transformations to support infinite loops and related constructs like break and continue.
To be specific:
lower_loop Function: This creates a control flow graph (CFG) for infinite loops by setting up an unconditional jump into the loop's header, executing the loop body, and terminating at an exit block. This ensures the block parameters align with the loop's binding set.
lower_while Updates: The exit block now correctly uses block parameters, fixing a prior bug. The new approach properly routes else_args in the header's Branch terminator, preserving loop-carried SSA through early exit paths.
SemanticStmt::Break and Continue: Both terms gather current SSA values for bindings, emitting a Jump terminator to respective targets (exit_id or header_id), effectively closing the block.
Adjusting lower_stmt: Now incorporates loop_ctx to handle nested control flow scenarios accurately. It fixes a critical issue where break within nested if statements didn’t resolve to the loop’s exit.
Error Handling: Constructs outside loops return UnsupportedSemanticConstruct, and missing SSA bindings throw InternalInvariantViolation.
Refactoring Tests: Existing tests got redirected from Loop to For, setting stage for upcoming improvements.
The structured break/continue commitment aligns with the roadmap, excluding labeled breaks for now—a post-0.1 concern.
Commit 03d2da6 introduces substantial documentation with AGENT_OPERATING_DOCTRINE.md, mapping out a six-phase workflow: Recon, Design Lock, Predicted Fa

zh.title: Cx 开发日志 — 2026-04-12
zh.summary: Cx 编译器项目通过新的 LoopContext 结构添加了对无限循环、break 和 continue 语句的支持，该结构正确管理控制流图和 SSA 绑定。这些更改修复了嵌套控制流处理中的关键错误，并将现有测试重定向向改进的语言功能。这些重大修改在三天开发期后等待集成到主分支中。

title: 6 MCP Servers for 3D & AR Development — What AI Can Build Now
summary: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open Anthropic standard enabling AI assistants to call specialized external tools for domain-specific code generation and validation. The author developed six MCP servers for 3D and AR development (SceneView, automotive, healthcare, gaming) that enable AI to generate compilable code with proper APIs and error handling. This approach bridges the gap between AI capabilities and production-ready implementation across specialized domains.
content:
What if you could ask Claude "build me a car configurator" and get compilable Android code in 10 seconds?
MCP (Model Context Protocol) makes this real. An MCP server gives AI assistants access to specialized tools — code generators, validators, domain knowledge — so they produce working code instead of generic suggestions.
I built 6 MCP servers for 3D and AR development. Here's what each one does and how to use them.
MCP is an open protocol (by Anthropic) that lets AI assistants call external tools. Think of it as plugins, but standardized — one server works with Claude, Cursor, Windsurf, and any MCP-compatible client.
An MCP server provides:
Tools: functions the AI can call (generate code, validate, search)
Resources: data the AI can read (API docs, samples)
Prompts: pre-built workflows (code review, scaffolding)
The flagship. Covers all SceneView features across Android, iOS, and Web.
npx sceneview-mcp

Key tools:
generate_scene — complete scene code from a description
validate_code — catches threading bugs, null handling, wrong APIs
search_models — searches Sketchfab for free 3D models
get_sample — returns working sample code for any feature
Example prompt: "Create a 3D product viewer with orbit camera and environment lighting"
npx automotive-3d-mcp

Specialized for automotive UX: car configurators, HUD overlays, 3D dashboards, AR showrooms, parts catalogs. Knows ADAS display patterns, Tier-1 integration requirements, and automotive UX standards.
Example prompt: "Build a car color configurator with paint + wheel options"
npx healthcare-3d-mcp

Anatomy viewers, DICOM slice rendering, molecular structures, surgical planning tools, dental scanning visualization. Includes HIPAA-compliant patterns and medical imaging standards.
Example prompt: "Create an interactive anatomy model with organ highlighting"
npx gaming-3d-mcp

Character controllers, terrain generation, particle systems, physics interactions, inventory UI, NPC behaviors. Covers casual games, not AAA — opti

zh.title: 6个用于3D和AR开发的MCP服务器——AI现在能构建什么
zh.summary: 模型上下文协议(MCP)是Anthropic开发的开放标准,使AI助手能够调用专门的外部工具进行特定领域的代码生成和验证。作者为3D和AR开发(SceneView、汽车、医疗、游戏)开发了6个MCP服务器,使AI能够生成具有适当API和错误处理的可编译代码。这种方法缩小了AI能力与专业领域生产就绪实现之间的差距。

title: TypeScript Utility Types That Actually Save Time in Production SaaS Code
summary: TypeScript utility types like Partial<T>, Pick<T, K>, Omit<T, K>, and Record<K, V> are essential patterns in production SaaS code for type-safe operations across database layers, APIs, and data serialization. Practical applications include patch operations with Omit, safe client data with Pick, and Stripe integrations where explicit types prevent security leaks and API mistakes. Combining these utilities creates self-documenting contracts between system components.

zh.title: 在生产SaaS代码中确实节省时间的TypeScript实用类型
zh.summary: TypeScript实用类型如Partial<T>、Pick<T, K>、Omit<T, K>和Record<K, V>是生产SaaS代码中必不可少的模式,用于数据库层、API和数据序列化的类型安全操作。实际应用包括使用Omit进行补丁操作、使用Pick进行安全客户端数据以及Stripe集成,其中显式类型可防止安全泄漏和API错误。结合这些实用程序可创建系统组件之间的自文档化契约。

title: The Only Compose-Native 3D & AR Library for Android
summary: SceneView is a Jetpack Compose library enabling declarative 3D and AR development on Android, filling the gap left by deprecated Sceneform and eliminating the need for heavy alternatives like Unity. Built on Google Filament engine with 26+ composable node types (ModelNode, SphereNode, VideoNode), it treats 3D objects as composables with state management matching standard Compose patterns. The library enables complex AR interactions without callback-heavy code.
content:
You're building an Android app with Jetpack Compose. You need 3D. Maybe a product viewer, a model configurator, or AR furniture placement.
You search. You ask ChatGPT. You try Copilot. And you get: Unity (50MB+, no Compose), raw Filament (1000 lines to render a cube), or Sceneform (deprecated since 2021).
There's a gap. Android has no built-in 3D composable. Until now.
SceneView treats 3D the same way Compose treats UI: declaratively. SceneView { } is a composable like Column { } or LazyList { }. Nodes are composables. State is Kotlin state.
implementation("io.github.sceneview:sceneview:4.0.0")

@Composable
fun ProductViewer() {
    val engine = rememberEngine()
    val modelLoader = rememberModelLoader(engine)

    SceneView(
        modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize(),
        engine = engine,
        modelLoader = modelLoader,
        cameraManipulator = rememberCameraManipulator()
    ) {
        rememberModelInstance(modelLoader, "models/sneaker.glb")?.let {
            ModelNode(modelInstance = it, scaleToUnits = 1.0f, autoAnimate = true)
        }
    }
}

Place sneaker.glb in src/main/assets/models/. The model loads asynchronously, rememberModelInstance returns null while loading, then triggers recomposition. No callbacks, no lifecycle — just state.
SceneView includes 26+ composable node types:
ModelNode — GLB/glTF models with animation
CubeNode, SphereNode, CylinderNode — primitive geometry
ImageNode — textured quads
VideoNode — video textures on 3D surfaces
LightNode — point, spot, directional lights
TextNode — 3D text billboards
ViewNode — any Compose UI in 3D space
All powered by Google Filament — the same PBR engine used in Google Maps and Android Auto.
implementation("io.github.sceneview:arsceneview:4.0.0")

@Composable
fun ARFurniturePlacement() {
    val engine = rememberEngine()
    val modelLoader = rememberModelLoader(engine)
    val anchors = remember { mutableStateListOf<Anchor>() }
    val chair = rememberModelInstance(modelLoader, "models/chair.

zh.title: Android上唯一的原生Compose 3D和AR库
zh.summary: SceneView是一个Jetpack Compose库,在Android上实现声明式3D和AR开发,填补了已弃用Sceneform留下的空白,无需使用Unity等重型替代方案。它基于Google Filament引擎构建,具有26+可组合节点类型(ModelNode、SphereNode、VideoNode),将3D对象视为具有与标准Compose模式相匹配的状态管理的可组合。该库可实现复杂的AR交互,而无需冗长的回调代码。

title: RGAA 4.1: France's Web Accessibility Standard Explained for Non-French Teams
summary: RGAA 4.1 is France's legally mandated web accessibility standard based on WCAG 2.1 but significantly stricter and prescriptive, becoming enforceable on private sector websites from June 2025. Unlike WCAG's descriptive guidelines, RGAA specifies exact tools and verification methods, creating compliance requirements distinct from international standards. Teams managing French websites must address RGAA criteria beyond standard WCAG AA compliance to avoid legal exposure.
content:
RGAA 4.1: France's Web Accessibility Standard Explained for Non-French Teams



Meta: RGAA is France's legally mandated accessibility framework (106 criteria). If your team manages French websites or serves French users, you must understand it. Here's what RGAA is, how it differs from WCAG, who's affected post-June 2025, and the 5 most commonly failed criteria with fixes.
Your team manages websites in France. Or you have French users. Or you're an international agency with a French client.
Someone on Slack asks: « What's the RGAA? Is that just WCAG with a French name? »
Not quite.
The RGAA (Référentiel Général d'Amélioration de l'Accessibilité) is France's legally binding accessibility standard. It's based on WCAG 2.1, but it's stricter, more prescriptive, and legally enforceable in France via the Défenseur des droits (Ombudsman) and, since June 2025, on private sector websites too.
If your French website isn't RGAA-compliant, you're exposed to legal complaints, public pressure, and—if you're large enough—pressure from the French government.
I've been working on accessibility for ten years. I've tested hundreds of websites across Europe. French websites in particular often miss these standards. Not malice. Just lack of clear resources in English.
This article is for teams who don't speak French, manage French users, and need to understand what's actually required.
WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) is the international standard from the W3C. It has three levels: A, AA, AAA. It covers contrast, keyboard navigation, alternative text for images, captions for video, semantic HTML—all the things that make a website usable for people with disabilities.
RGAA is a French translation and adaptation of WCAG 2.1, but with one key difference: it's prescriptive, not descriptive.
WCAG says: « Check the contrast ratio of your text. »
RGAA says: « Use axe DevTools or WAVE to measure contrast. The minimum is 4.5:1 for normal text. Here's exactly how to verify it. »
WCAG lea

zh.title: RGAA 4.1：为非法语团队详解法国网络无障碍标准
zh.summary: RGAA 4.1是法国基于WCAG 2.1制定的法律规定的网络无障碍标准，但更为严格和规范，自2025年6月起对私营部门网站强制执行。与WCAG的描述性指南不同，RGAA规定了具体的工具和验证方法，建立了与国际标准不同的合规要求。管理法国网站的团队必须满足RGAA标准，超越标准WCAG AA合规，以规避法律风险。

title: I'm Not a Community Person. I'm Starting a Community Project Anyway.
summary: A software engineer launched FluxHelp, a free community tech help initiative to bridge the gap between technology experts and non-technical users struggling with basic digital tasks. The project emphasizes teaching rather than just fixing, enabling users to solve future problems independently rather than repeatedly paying for service calls. This addresses a real access gap in affordable, judgment-free technical support in the community.
content:
I'm not a community person. I say that without shame. It's just how I'm wired. My energy is finite, my brain is protective of it, and most of my life has been spent in front of a screen solving problems nobody asked me to solve. I show up to things when I have to. I leave when I can. I've never been the person who joins committees or volunteers at events or posts in the neighborhood Facebook group.
So naturally, I'm starting a free tech help initiative in Springfield. Obviously.
I work in IT. I've been around technology my whole life. Building things, breaking things, figuring out how things work since I was twelve. I went to school for it. Got degrees in it. Taught classes in it. And the one constant across all of that is the gap between people who understand technology and people who need it to function in daily life.
That gap isn't about intelligence. It's about access. It's about having someone you can ask who won't make you feel stupid for asking.
I've watched people pay $200 to get a printer reconnected to WiFi. I've seen someone almost fall for a scam popup because they didn't know those weren't real warnings. I've talked to people who just need someone to sit with them for twenty minutes and explain where the setting is. But that twenty minutes doesn't exist in their world, because every option costs money or dignity or both.
FluxHelp is me offering what I already do for friends and family: tech help, no charge, no judgment, no "well actually."
But here's the part that matters to me. This isn't just "hand me your laptop and I'll fix it." I mean, I'll absolutely do that if that's what you need. But I'd rather teach you what's happening and why, so next time you can handle it yourself. Or at least know what questions to ask.
I actually taught college classes for a bit. C# and Android development. I liked the explaining part, but I'll be honest, it left me frustrated. Most of the students were there because someone told them tech jobs pay well. They weren't stu

zh.title: 我不是社区人士。但我还是在启动一个社区项目。
zh.summary: 一名软件工程师启动了FluxHelp，这是一个免费的社区技术帮助计划，旨在缩小技术专家与在基本数字任务上困难的非技术用户之间的差距。该项目强调教学而不仅仅是修复，使用户能够独立解决未来的问题，而不是反复支付技术支持费用。这解决了社区中负担得起、无评判的技术支持的可及性差距。
